Because what causes a prospect to say, 'We're good,' or 'We're not interested,' or 'We already have somebody for that' at the beginning of a sales conversation? When I say emotional, that means the emotional connection that you have with your prospect, okay?
Do human beings buy on logic or emotion? Emotion, 100%. There's no debate in brain science on this, okay? So if we can't help our prospects relive their pain of their current situation, their past history, and then have a fear, that's an emotion, a fear of future pain, they feel no need to what? To change. And that's why you get lots of objections. That's why they ultimately don't buy from you, okay? So in order to influence at the highest level, Tony Robbins talks a lot about this. I always take it a step further. In order to... emotionally connect. You have to master how to emotionally connect with them where they feel like you understand them, their needs, their desires, their fears, their wants without buying into their story.
Their story could be: 'I need to think it over. I need to do more research. I need to keep looking around. Call me back later when I'm not so busy, or I need to talk to my spouse. To be sold to consumers.' That's a story. If I buy into that story, I offer them no value. I cannot solve their problems and get them where they want if they do not buy. connection. I have to go there first where they feel like I understand them. I'm going there first. It's an emotional connection. You don't get an emotional connection by asking. surface-level, vague, generalized questions like, 'What are some challenges you're having?' or 'What keeps you awake at night?' or 'Can you tell me two problems?' Those are surface-level questions.
Your prospect knows where those questions lead, okay? This is the key to NEPQ, the emotional... Connection is where the human being lets their guard down and becomes open to what you're offering. That's where change happens. Okay, persuasion. Now, this is how you frame the question. So, how do you frame your offer? Whoever frames their solution best will always make the sale. Now, what do I mean by frames? Best. Let me give you an example. Would you say Jeffrey Bezos was the only person that came up with the concept like Amazon? Unlikely. There's probably other people that thought about that. Or had it out there, but Jeffrey made history. That's why he has his company because he was able to frame it better. He was better at communicating his offer.
That's why he's there. Elon Musk, was he the only person that had the concept of the electrical car? Unlikely. He got to where he was at because he knew how to frame. His solution and offer better. So that's what I'm talking about. So let's say that you have a you have a family picture. So imagine. Your family picture up on your your mantle at your house. You've got your your spouse, your kids, or maybe your mom, your dad, your brothers, your sisters, whatever. You got this frame, this picture. You got two pictures, same exact picture. You go to the store, you buy two separate frames. Okay, one frame is like immaculate. It's like gold— just looks the the most beautiful frame ever. You put that around that family picture.
The next frame you go to, like a flea market, and it's got holes in it. It's a cheap frame. It looks like just— just looks bad. It's got cobwebs on it. Just beat-up frame. And you put it around that same family picture. Which one are you gonna choose? The one that's framed better. That looks nicer. The frame looks better. It's better. Same picture, but because of the frame, you chose that option. So persuasion stands for: I'm framing my offer much better. I'm actually getting them to persuade themselves. That's called self-persuasion. Rather than me telling you why you should do something, what if you learned how to get your prospect to tell themselves why they should do something, which is more persuasive? Let's get back to the training video and then questioning. Okay, so how am I reframing the questions to interrupt your prospects' pattern? Let me give an example. For example, what are prospects used to from salespeople? Like a standard question. Can you tell me two problems you're having? Can you give me some challenges that keep you awake at night?
See, like I said earlier, they know where these questions lead. They recognize the pattern. Your brain recognizes patterns. So does your prospect. So I need to... re-language those type of questions. So let's say, if I sold life insurance and you find out that they have a $50,000 work policy. Instead of you saying, 'Well, what do you feel like you need for coverage?' or 'Why do you feel like you need coverage?' Because they know where that question leads. I might say, 'So you've already got the $50,000 work policy.' What's caused you to feel like? That might not be enough. Well, don't get me wrong, it's good, but and then they tell you why they feel like what they have is not enough.
But more importantly, who did they just tell themselves, self-persuasion. See how I'm reframing the questions to interrupt the pattern, okay? Or let's say that you sell for a marketing agency to a company. I might reframe the question. And rather than saying, 'Can you tell me some problems that you're having with your current leads?' I might say, 'So you've been using XYZ vendor for the past five years.' I mean, it can't be all doom and gloom. What do you like about what they've done? Well, we like this. We like that. What would you change about them if you could, though? Well, don't get me wrong. I mean, we like them, but see, I set it up by basically. I mean, it can't be all doom and gloom over there.
What have they done well so far? So they're not used to that pattern. A few examples of reframing your questions. I could show you about 10 billion other things to do. So every question I developed within APQ does one of four things. Okay. And I want you to write this down. Every question I have developed, every industry first, is designed to seed doubt about the prospect, current state, or their current situation. Let's say, if you're selling sass, like, and you sell some type of thing that automates something for a company and they have to man do it, and you find out when you're going through, like, finding out what their situation is, that they have to do it manually, I might lean and say, 'Oh, so how long have they been forcing you to have to do it manually for seeing my facial expression.' And did you hear my tongue?
How long have they been forcing you guys to have to do it manually for? Okay. I'm seeding doubt. Okay. That maybe I know something that they don't have to be forced to do it manually that they don't know. Okay. So I strictly use my tone also. Any PQ, every question I'm asking is designed to also prevent objections from happening in their mind. So let's say, if you get a lot of spouse objections because you sell to consumers. Sold network marketing: you help them start their own business, they can make more money, have more time with their family, and let's say the prospect says, 'Oh my gosh, you know I work 75 hours a week. I'm going to lean in and say, so how does your spouse feel about you having to work 75 hours a week?' See, if I want to prevent the spouse's objection, I have to somehow get the spouse involved in the problem and the consequences, right?
How does she feel about you having to work 75 hours a week? Oh, she doesn't like it, blah, blah, blah. Does she want you to find something from home where you can have time with her? Oh, definitely, she does, blah, blah. See, I'm starting to prevent that objection from happening. Now there's more to that, but that's the start of getting that out of their mind. Okay. Now, another reason why every NABQ question I've is also designed to build the gap from where they are to where they want to be. Okay. I don't want to help them just find one problem because they might feel like they can solve that by themselves. But my questions, my tonality, how I do that, I want to help them find two or three or four or five other problems they didn't realize they actually have.
So let's say, for example, we train a huge precious metals company out of California, a couple of them actually. And let's say that a prospect gets on the phone and they say, 'Well, I'm looking to maybe invest in gold and silver because I want to protect myself against inflation.' Oh, well, that's a big reason why people come to us. Let me tell you how we do that. And you sell to that one problem. What happens if they go home, they turn on Fox News or some news station? They're like, 'Yeah, with the current administration, inflation is going down.' Then they might come back and say, 'Well, we don't really need gold or silver because inflation's going down.' See, because you only sold to one problem.
I want to help them find two, three, four, five other problems where that gap's so big that even if they feel like that other... Problem doesn't exist, there's four other problems that they still have that they know they can't solve themselves. That's called building the gap. And then the fourth thing that every NPQ question is designed to do is to get them into that future state where I take them out of price or cost-based thinking into results-based thinking. Remember, you're not selling the thing, you're selling the result. Results of what your thing does. Okay. So if you're a business consulting, you're not selling consulting, you're selling the results, what your consulting does, which is probably to drive more revenue and more profit and to scale the company.
If you're a marketing agency, you're not selling leads. You're selling actually what? Better leads so they can grow the business, make more money and scale, right? If you're, let's say, a life insurance agent, you're not selling them a policy. You're selling them the results of what happens when one spouse dies and the other spouse is financially protected forever. That's what you're selling. All right. So NEPQ designed to do those four things. Now. You might not be aware of this, but like I said, I went to school for neuropsychology. Behavioral science is going to become a psychologist. And we are taught every human being you talk to, salesperson, and you talk to human beings has a frame. That's a belief system. So every human being you're gonna talk to, every prospect has belief systems about you, the salesperson, right?
Would you say? That society in large views you as a high status or do they view salespeople at a low status? They low status. It's just the way it works. Now the question is why? Society's conditioned everybody to view salespeople at a lower status. Because what do you view a doctor as? Higher status, because that's how society teaches you to view them, right? But if you really think about it, why wouldn't... Salespeople view themselves as being in a higher status because, if nothing is sold, that means there's no such thing as an economy, and who are the ones that sell things? You do the sales. And so, you would think you would be higher status because, if you don't sell anything, there's no economy.
If there's no economy, there's no such thing as what we call a society. Are you getting what I'm throwing down here? Okay? So every human being has a belief system, all right? So belief systems are typically brought about from our childhood, from the ages of four to 13, by the people we raised, our parents, maybe the cousins we hung out with, the church we went to. If you're a kid now, even the influencers you follow on TikTok, they are triggering your worldview or your belief system or your current way of thinking. So what is a belief system that... pretty much you have anytime a salesperson first starts talking to you. What's the first thing that comes to your mind when a salesperson first starts talking to you?
How much is this gonna cost? Now, why does your brain go there? Because you've been conditioned, you have that belief system, okay? So that's called cost-based thinking. Do I want my prospect to think about the cost the whole time I'm talking to him? Unlikely. I want them to think about the 'what?' The end result. So how do I take them out of that frame or that way of thinking? That is called a deframe. Okay. So I've got to deframe them out of that. And then I want to reframe. That's the process. Okay. So reframing is the process. How do I reframe? Reframe them into a new belief system or a new frame about the results of what I do, not the cost. OK, so that is what any PQ does.
